title: NYC Yellow Taxi 2017 — Exploratory Data Analysis
summary: A structured EDA on 22,699 NYC taxi trips, uncovering data-quality issues, tipping behavior, and a strong distance-fare correlation, with separate technical and executive reports.
problem: A sample of 22,699 2017 NYC Yellow Taxi trips needed structured exploratory analysis to validate assumptions about fare structure, ridership, and tipping, surface time-based demand patterns, and quantify how strongly trip distance predicts fare — using the PACE framework (Plan, Analyze, Construct, Execute).
approach: Cleaned and engineered the dataset in Python (Pandas, NumPy), converting timestamps and deriving trip duration, month, and day-of-week features. Ran univariate analysis (box plots + histograms) on trip distance, fare, and tip amount, then bivariate analysis comparing tipping by vendor and passenger count, ride/revenue trends by day and month, and a distance-vs-fare correlation. Delivered findings as two separate reports: a technical report with full methodology for analysts, and a plain-language executive summary with business recommendations for stakeholders.
results: Found a strong positive correlation (r = 0.767) between trip distance and total fare, confirmed the median (1.61 mi) is more representative than the mean for typical trip length due to right-skew, and identified that 35.5% of trips received no tip at all. Also flagged real data-quality issues — 14 trips with negative fares and a $1,200 outlier fare — and recommended an outlier-review step before any revenue reporting. Demand peaked on Fridays by ride count but Thursdays by revenue, with March the busiest month and July the quietest.
